posts.text: This is very “me” humor. 😊

And the '70s & '80s saw the collapse of the newsstand distribution model & the rise of the direct market.

And who's going to go to a comic shop every month to pick up their books? The dedicated fans.
posts.parent_text:
Subscriptions were always a tiny segment of sales, but they help measure dedicated fans

Low subscription numbers suggests that the books were probably being bought as impulse purchases at supermarket checkouts & corner stores. It probably wasn't someone going to pick up their monthly copy of Ghosts

posts.text:
It’s called “model collapse”. Basically, non expert users lower the quality of the learning set.

The models only produce usable lines of code about half the time. They try to improve by tracking how output is edited.

Chat bots exhibit the same thing:

www.newscientist.com/article/2479...

“[T]he noise that these rich, reckless bunglers create makes it harder to _know things_. Their mess shits up the search engines; their AI spins stupid new lies to life by haplessly plagiarizing and re-plagiarizing itself, eating its own excretions.” defector.com/scorn-illust...
